metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify empty-string semantics in the plan (e.g. 'an empty string is treated as absent and the resolver continues to the next var') so the unit test has a deterministic expected value.
findings.finding: The unit-test AC mentions 'empty-string handling' but never states the expected behavior — whether an empty-string value is treated as absent (skipped) or as a valid session-id. This is a real design decision, not an implementation detail, and the executor must invent the answer.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria – 'Unit tests cover: each var precedence, empty-string handling, all-absent -> None'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What section: 'An env var set to the empty string (export REBAR_SESSION_ID="") is treated as absent; the resolver skips it and continues down the list.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Tighten AC4 to explicitly state its scope: e.g. 'The shared helper never returns git HEAD; the FORCE_CLOSE audit comment records `None`/empty rather than git HEAD when no session var is set' (or explicitly permit the cosmetic fallback with a scope qualifier).
findings.finding: AC4 ('Never returns git HEAD as the session id') does not specify its scope — it is ambiguous whether it constrains only the return value of `resolve_session_id()`, or also the value ultimately written to the FORCE_CLOSE audit comment. The What section describes a local cosmetic fallback that would still produce HEAD in the audit comment, making the criterion's pass/fail indeterminate.
findings.location: AC4

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the 'What' section (keeps HEAD/unknown fallback locally) with AC4 ('Never returns git HEAD as the session id') and the bug framing in 'Why' — either explicitly permit HEAD as a cosmetic display fallback (narrowing AC4 to apply only to the resolver's return value) or remove the HEAD fallback entirely and update 'What' accordingly.
findings.finding: The 'Why' section explicitly labels git HEAD appearing in the FORCE_CLOSE audit comment as a bug to fix, but the 'What' section says 'The FORCE_CLOSE call site keeps its cosmetic HEAD/"unknown" fallback locally' — meaning the audit comment can still contain git HEAD when the session id is absent. This directly contradicts AC4 ('Never returns git HEAD as the session id') if AC4 is read as applying to the full audit comment value, not just the resolver's return.
findings.location: Why section vs What section vs AC4
findings.suggested_fix: Narrow AC4 to: 'The shared `resolve_session_id()` helper never returns git HEAD (returns None on absence); cosmetic fallback in FORCE_CLOSE display logic remains HEAD/unknown but is not treated as a session id.' This aligns all three sections.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly acknowledge in the plan that the precedence inversion for `session_log` (CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID was first, REBAR_SESSION_ID becomes first) is an intentional breaking behavioral change, and add a note on expected user impact or a rollback path if the change is found to be incorrect.
findings.finding: The refactor changes the precedence order for the `session_log` call site: the current resolver puts `CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID` before `REBAR_SESSION_ID`, but the new shared resolver inverts this (`REBAR_SESSION_ID` first). This is a behavioral change for any user who has both vars set simultaneously. The plan does not acknowledge this as a deliberate breaking change or provide a rollback path.
findings.location: Why section (existing session_log resolver order) vs What section (new shared resolver order)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the 'What' or 'Scope' section: 'Note: this changes session_log precedence — REBAR_SESSION_ID now wins over CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID when both are set; this is intentional and users relying on the old order should migrate to REBAR_SESSION_ID.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge and justify the precedence inversion at the `session_log` call site (old: `CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID` first; new: `REBAR_SESSION_ID` first), and confirm no existing callers rely on the old ordering — or explicitly accept the behavior change.
findings.finding: The new shared precedence order (`REBAR_SESSION_ID` first) silently REVERSES the existing `session_log` resolver's behavior, which previously put `CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID` before `REBAR_SESSION_ID`. Any caller that sets both vars will now resolve to a different session id after the refactor. The plan does not acknowledge this behavioral inversion or assess its impact on existing session-log consumers.
findings.location: ## What — ordered var list; ## Acceptance Criteria — AC2 ('session_log … calls the shared helper')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the 'What' or 'Scope' section: 'Note: the new ordering promotes REBAR_SESSION_ID above CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID at the session_log call site (previously CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID had higher priority there); confirm no active consumers depend on the old order before merging.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge and justify the precedence-order change for `session_log` (currently `CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID` > `REBAR_SESSION_ID`; proposed shared order reverses this), or add an AC stating the new order is intentional.
findings.finding: The plan's proposed shared resolver order (REBAR_SESSION_ID → CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID → …) silently demotes CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID relative to REBAR_SESSION_ID in session_log, which today puts CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID FIRST (session_log.py:56-57). The plan never acknowledges this behavioral change to session_log's fingerprint logic, meaning the refactor may spuriously break Claude Code session log rotation for users who set both vars.
findings.location: Plan 'What' section — shared resolver order; src/rebar/_commands/session_log.py:56-61
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note in the plan 'What' section: 'Note: session_log currently places CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID before REBAR_SESSION_ID; the new shared order reverses this — verify no rotation-regression for users with both vars set, or keep CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID before REBAR_SESSION_ID in the shared list.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly acknowledge (and justify) the precedence inversion for `session_log`: under the unified chain `REBAR_SESSION_ID` beats `CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID`, reversing today's `session_log` behavior where `CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID` wins — note this in the plan's What/Scope or add a migration note.
findings.finding: The plan proposes a unified precedence `REBAR_SESSION_ID -> CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID -> ...` but the current `session_log._resolve_session_fp` uses `CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID -> REBAR_SESSION_ID -> SESSION_ID` (CLAUDE_CODE wins over REBAR today). The plan does not acknowledge this is a behavioral inversion for `session_log` — any user running inside Claude Code with REBAR_SESSION_ID also set will get a different fingerprint after the change, potentially triggering unwanted session-log rotation.
findings.location: What section (ordered list); src/rebar/_commands/session_log.py:47-61

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly acknowledge (in the plan's 'What' or 'Acceptance Criteria') that the unified precedence order changes `session_log`'s existing behavior when both `REBAR_SESSION_ID` and `CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID` are set, and either confirm the inversion is intentional or adjust the unified order to preserve the current `session_log` behavior (`CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID` before `REBAR_SESSION_ID`), adding a test that asserts the winner when both vars are set.
findings.finding: The plan's unified precedence order (`REBAR_SESSION_ID` first, then `CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID`) silently reverses the current precedence in `session_log._resolve_session_fp` (which puts `CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID` first). When both vars are set simultaneously, the existing call site today resolves to `CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID`; the unified resolver will resolve to `REBAR_SESSION_ID`. This is a behavioral change at the session_log call site that the plan does not acknowledge, and the existing test suite (which always deletes `REBAR_SESSION_ID` before setting `CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID`) won't catch the regression.
findings.location: ## What — ordered var list; src/rebar/_commands/session_log.py:_resolve_session_fp (current precedence); Acceptance Criteria — unit tests
findings.suggested_fix: If the intent is that the explicit rebar-owned var always wins everywhere, state that decision in the plan and add an AC test: `REBAR_SESSION_ID set + CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID set -> REBAR_SESSION_ID wins`. If the intent is to preserve existing session_log behavior, reorder to `CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID -> REBAR_SESSION_ID -> …` (matching the current session_log code) and note that transition_close's FORCE_CLOSE path gets CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID for the first time as a side effect of the unified order.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly acknowledge in the plan that the new shared order demotes `CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID` relative to `REBAR_SESSION_ID` for `session_log` (reversing current behavior), and either justify this as intentional or restore `CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID` to first position in the ordered list.
findings.finding: The plan's shared resolver silently reverses the existing `session_log` precedence: today `session_log._resolve_session_fp` puts `CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID` BEFORE `REBAR_SESSION_ID`, but the new shared resolver puts `REBAR_SESSION_ID` FIRST — a behavioral change for users who set both. The plan provides no rationale, migration note, or acknowledgment of this behavioral delta.
findings.location: What section ordered list vs. `src/rebar/_commands/session_log.py` lines 57-59
findings.suggested_fix: If `REBAR_SESSION_ID` is meant to be an explicit override for all call sites, keep it first and add a one-sentence rationale. If `CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID` should remain first for session_log specifically, specify a site-specific override rather than a single shared list.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a proving command for each call-site AC: e.g. `grep -R 'resolve_session_id' src/rebar/session_log.py src/rebar/transition_close.py` returns matches for both files, plus a pytest test that patches `CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID` and asserts the FORCE_CLOSE audit record contains that value.
findings.finding: No AC has a concrete proving command; in particular AC2 ('both call sites call the shared helper') and AC3 ('FORCE_CLOSE audit captures CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID') have no described test or grep/pytest invocation that would produce evidence of correctness — only the implicit claim that 'unit tests pass'.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria – ACs 2, 3, and 4
findings.suggested_fix: Add to ACs: 'A pytest test in test_transition_close.py sets CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID=test-id, triggers FORCE_CLOSE, and asserts the audit comment contains test-id (not a git SHA or "unknown").' Also add: 'grep (or AST check) confirms no local resolver chain remains in session_log.py or transition_close.py.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion (or named test) that directly exercises `transition_close` / the FORCE_CLOSE path with `CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID` set, confirming the shared resolver is actually wired in and the correct id appears in the emitted audit record.
findings.finding: The test plan covers the shared helper in isolation but specifies no test that verifies the call-site wiring — i.e., that `transition_close` (the buggy site) actually invokes the shared resolver and therefore captures `CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID`. A correctly-implemented helper with a missed wiring step would pass all specified unit tests while leaving the FORCE_CLOSE bug unfixed.
findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria — AC3 ('FORCE_CLOSE audit now captures CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID when present'), AC5 (unit tests)
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: 'An integration/unit test for transition_close verifies that when CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID is set and REBAR_SESSION_ID is absent, the FORCE_CLOSE audit record contains the CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID value (not git HEAD or unknown).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly name `docs/config.md` lines 336-343 as a required edit (not just a target for new text) to replace the existing stale precedence description with the new unified chain.
findings.finding: `docs/config.md` already documents the session-provenance contract at lines 336-343 using the OLD precedence (`REBAR_SESSION_ID → SESSION_ID → short git HEAD → 'unknown'`), with no mention of `CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID`. The plan's AC says to document the ordered contract in `docs/config.md (or event-schema)` as if the section might not exist, but it does exist and must be overwritten. This stale prose is an unenumerated edit target that could cause the docs AC to be checked off while leaving incorrect documentation in place.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: '`docs/config.md` (or event-schema) documents the ordered contract'; docs/config.md:336-343
findings.suggested_fix: Change the AC to: 'Update `docs/config.md` §Session-provenance (lines 336-343) to replace the old chain with the new unified `REBAR_SESSION_ID → CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID → OPENCODE_SESSION_ID → CODEX_THREAD_ID → SESSION_ID → None` contract.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the canonical target file (e.g. `docs/config.md`) rather than offering an 'or' alternative, so the executor has a single deliverable.
findings.finding: The docs AC says 'docs/config.md (or event-schema)' — the 'or' leaves the target file as an open choice for the executor rather than a stated default, making the docs deliverable ambiguous.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria – '`docs/config.md` (or event-schema) documents the ordered contract'
findings.suggested_fix: Change to: '`docs/config.md` documents the ordered env-var contract (add a dedicated "Session ID resolution" section).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the 'docs/config.md (or event-schema)' ambiguity by designating a single canonical location for the ordered env-var contract (recommend docs/config.md) and explicitly noting in the AC whether the event-schema also needs a cross-reference.
findings.finding: The documentation target for the ordered env-var contract is specified as 'docs/config.md (or event-schema)' — a genuine either/or that leaves the implementer free to pick either location (or stop after one). If the event-schema is chosen, docs/config.md is left without the new contract; if config.md is chosen, the event-schema may still reference a stale or incomplete session-id section. Neither location is definitively assigned, so the AC can be satisfied without both being correct.
findings.location: Scope / AC: '`docs/config.md` (or event-schema) documents the ordered contract'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the AC to: '`docs/config.md` documents the ordered env-var contract (REBAR_SESSION_ID → … → None); if the event schema defines a session-id field, add a one-line cross-reference there as well.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Trim OPENCODE_SESSION_ID and CODEX_THREAD_ID from the resolver's ordered list (and the 'data-driven array' extensibility rationale) until at least one of them has a confirmed usage or a current AC requires them — the bug fix only needs `REBAR_SESSION_ID → CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID → SESSION_ID → None`.
findings.finding: The plan adds OPENCODE_SESSION_ID and CODEX_THREAD_ID to the resolver's ordered list and motivates the 'data-driven array' design specifically for future extensibility ('new harness vars are a one-line add'), but neither variable appears anywhere in the codebase (code, tests, docs). Including them and designing the abstraction for extensibility serves a hypothetical future, not the current story's definition of done (which only requires fixing the CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID omission in FORCE_CLOSE).
findings.location: What section: ordered var list `REBAR_SESSION_ID -> CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID -> OPENCODE_SESSION_ID -> CODEX_THREAD_ID -> SESSION_ID -> None`; also 'The list is a single data-driven array so new harness vars are a one-line add.'
findings.suggested_fix: Reduce the ordered list to `REBAR_SESSION_ID → CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID → SESSION_ID → None` for this story. Document the list as 'ordered by precedence' without the 'one-line add' extensibility framing. Add OPENCODE_SESSION_ID / CODEX_THREAD_ID in a follow-on story once the actual var names exported by those runtimes are confirmed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify OPENCODE_SESSION_ID and CODEX_THREAD_ID against official OpenCode / OpenAI Codex runtime documentation or source before committing them to the ordered list.
findings.finding: The plan asserts OPENCODE_SESSION_ID and CODEX_THREAD_ID as the correct env var names exported by the OpenCode and OpenAI/Codex runtimes respectively. No repo evidence (code, docs, tests, survey notes) corroborates these specific names; neither appears anywhere in the codebase.
findings.location: Plan 'What' section — ordered var list: 'OPENCODE_SESSION_ID -> CODEX_THREAD_ID'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_completion_gate.py` (lines 477-504) as an explicit edit target — the four `_resolve_session` unit tests must be retired or repointed to the new shared `resolve_session_id()` helper.
findings.finding: Four existing tests in `test_completion_gate.py` (lines 477-504) directly call the private `_tc._resolve_session` function. After the refactor removes or replaces this function with the shared helper, these tests will break. The plan's Scope section only mentions writing new unit tests, without acknowledging that these existing tests must be updated or deleted.
findings.location: Scope section (IN: unit tests); tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_completion_gate.py:477-504
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope IN list: 'retire/update the four `_tc._resolve_session` unit tests in `test_completion_gate.py` that directly call the private function (they will be superseded by the new shared-helper precedence tests).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Provide a cited source (OpenCode/Codex documentation, OSS repo, or empirical test) confirming `OPENCODE_SESSION_ID` and `CODEX_THREAD_ID` are the correct env var names exported by those runtimes before encoding them in the resolver.
findings.finding: `OPENCODE_SESSION_ID` and `CODEX_THREAD_ID` are named in the unified resolver list but do not appear anywhere in the existing codebase (source, tests, or docs). The plan provides no reference or citation confirming these are the actual env vars exported by the OpenCode and Codex runtimes respectively. This is a potential hallucination of edit targets — if the var names are wrong, the resolver silently skips the intended harness.
findings.location: What section: 'REBAR_SESSION_ID -> CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID -> OPENCODE_SESSION_ID -> CODEX_THREAD_ID -> SESSION_ID -> None'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (with a source link, OSS code search, or documented spike result) that `OPENCODE_SESSION_ID` and `CODEX_THREAD_ID` are the exact env var names exported by those runtimes before committing them to the resolver contract.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `OPENCODE_SESSION_ID` and `CODEX_THREAD_ID` as the actual env var names exported by the OpenCode and OpenAI Codex CLI runtimes respectively, but no source citation, documentation link, or survey artifact exists anywhere in the repo to verify these names. A repo-wide search returns zero hits for either name outside cached plan-review files. If either name is wrong, the resolver silently skips that harness with no error — the AC 'each var precedence' unit test will pass against the wrong name, and field attribution for those harnesses silently fails.
findings.location: What section — ordered resolver list: `OPENCODE_SESSION_ID -> CODEX_THREAD_ID`
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief evidence table to the plan (one row per harness: harness name | env var | source link | natively exported?). For any harness where the var is not natively exported, note 'shim required' — mirroring the existing Claude Code local-CLI caveat already present in the codebase.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify `OPENCODE_SESSION_ID` and `CODEX_THREAD_ID` against the OpenCode and Codex runtime documentation/source before hard-coding them in the ordered list (both are currently Unverified); if the correct names differ, update the list, or add a comment citing the verification source.
findings.finding: The env var names `OPENCODE_SESSION_ID` and `CODEX_THREAD_ID` are asserted as the actual variables exported by the OpenCode and Codex (OpenAI) runtimes respectively, but no source, documentation, or prior code in the repo confirms these are the real names those harnesses export. If either name is wrong, the resolver silently falls through to a lower-precedence var or None for users of those runtimes — a silent correctness failure.
findings.location: What section — ordered list: `REBAR_SESSION_ID -> CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID -> OPENCODE_SESSION_ID -> CODEX_THREAD_ID -> SESSION_ID -> None`
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief inline comment in the plan (and the eventual code) citing the primary source (e.g. OpenCode repo env-var docs, OpenAI Codex API reference) that confirms each var name. If the names cannot be verified before implementation, scope them OUT of the initial ordered list and add them as a follow-up once confirmed — the plan already says 'new harness vars are a one-line add', so deferral is low-cost.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a source or reference (OSS docs link, env-var survey, or inline comment) to the plan confirming that `OPENCODE_SESSION_ID` and `CODEX_THREAD_ID` are the actual runtime-exported variable names for OpenCode and Codex respectively, or remove them from the ordered list until verified.
findings.finding: OPENCODE_SESSION_ID and CODEX_THREAD_ID are named in the plan's ordered resolver list as canonical env-var names for OpenCode and Codex runtimes, but grep across the entire repo (src/, docs/, tests/) finds zero occurrences of either name — no code, no docs, no test, no OSS reference confirms these are the actual variable names exported by those runtimes.
findings.location: What section — ordered list: `REBAR_SESSION_ID -> CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID -> OPENCODE_SESSION_ID -> CODEX_THREAD_ID -> SESSION_ID -> None`
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `## Harness var provenance` subsection to the plan citing the source (e.g. opencode GitHub repo env-var definitions, OpenAI Codex docs) for each non-Claude var. If unverified, omit them from the initial ordered list and note them as a follow-on.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact local fallback pattern at the FORCE_CLOSE call site (e.g. `resolve_session_id() or _short_head(tracker) or 'unknown'`) in the What section, and explicitly call out that `docs/config.md` lines 336-343 must be updated to reflect the new full precedence chain including `CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID`.
findings.finding: The plan splits the decision rule for the HEAD/unknown fallback across two locations — the shared `resolve_session_id()` returns None, and the FORCE_CLOSE call site 'keeps its cosmetic HEAD/unknown fallback locally' — but does not specify what that local code looks like, leaving the executor to re-invent the exact `or _short_head(tracker) or 'unknown'` pattern without a spec.
findings.location: What section: 'The FORCE_CLOSE call site keeps its cosmetic HEAD/"unknown" fallback locally, but the shared resolver returns None on absence.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
